title: How to Earn 100,000 Aeroplan Points in 12 Months Without Flying
excerpt: A practical roadmap for Canadian households to build a 100,000 Aeroplan balance in one year using credit card spending alone — no flights required.
heroImageAlt: Person reviewing a financial plan at a desk with a coffee
metaDescription: How to earn 100,000 Aeroplan points in 12 months without flying. A Canadian household strategy using credit cards, welcome bonuses, and everyday spending.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: 100,000 Aeroplan points in 12 months is realistic for most Canadian households · Welcome bonuses alone can get you 60–70% of the way there · The Amex Cobalt is the single best card for ongoing Aeroplan accumulation · 100K points = ~$2,000 in business class travel value

One hundred thousand Aeroplan points sounds like a lot. It is — it's enough for a one-way business class seat to Europe worth $4,000+ in cash, or a round-trip economy ticket to Japan. But for a Canadian household spending normally on credit cards, it's entirely achievable in 12 months. Here's the exact math.

content.heading: The Fast Lane: Welcome Bonuses

content.body: Welcome bonuses are the single biggest lever in this strategy. A well-chosen first card can hand you 50,000–70,000 points before you've spent a single dollar on anything other than meeting the minimum spend. The Amex Platinum currently offers 70,000 Membership Rewards points after spending $10,000 in three months — which transfers to Aeroplan at 1:1. That's 70% of your 100K target from one card. The TD Aeroplan Visa Infinite offers 20,000 Aeroplan points with its welcome offer plus a first-year fee rebate. Stack both and you're already at 90,000 before the year is out.

content.heading: Everyday Spending: The Amex Cobalt Strategy

content.body: The Amex Cobalt earns 5x Membership Rewards points on dining and groceries — and those MR points transfer to Aeroplan at 1:1. A Canadian household spending $1,200/month on food categories (a realistic figure for a family of three) earns 6,000 Amex MR points per month, or 72,000 per year. After transferring to Aeroplan, that's 72,000 miles from grocery and restaurant spending alone, on top of any welcome bonus. At a $155.88 annual fee, the cost-per-point earned is well under 0.3 cents — exceptional value.

content.heading: Complementary Cards to Fill the Gaps

items: TD Aeroplan Visa Infinite: 1.5x on groceries, gas, Air Canada — earns directly into Aeroplan
CIBC Aeroplan Visa Infinite: mirror of TD offer, good as a household second card
Amex Gold Rewards: 2x on dining and grocery — lower than Cobalt but strong earn on travel (3x)
Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite: earns Scene+ (not Aeroplan) but no FX fees save money on overseas spend

content.heading: The 12-Month Accumulation Plan

rows: TD Aeroplan Visa Infinite welcome bonus
Depends on fare class and distance

content.heading: When to Transfer Amex MR to Aeroplan

content.body: Don't transfer Amex MR points to Aeroplan until you have a specific redemption in mind. Keep them in MR for flexibility — MR points can go to Aeroplan, Avios, Flying Blue, or other programs. The moment you know exactly what you want to book and have confirmed award availability, transfer only what you need. If Amex is running a transfer bonus (historically 15–30% extra to Aeroplan, offered 2–4 times per year), that's the ideal moment to move a large balance.

content.heading: Common Pitfalls to Avoid

items: Applying for multiple cards at once — space applications 6 months apart to protect your credit score
Carrying a balance — credit card interest destroys every cent of points value
Letting Aeroplan points expire — keep the account active with at least one transaction every 18 months
Redeeming for merchandise or statement credits — always redeem for flights to get 2x+ the value

content.heading: Pro tip: Use grocery gift cards to hit Amex Platinum minimum spend
content.body: To meet the Amex Platinum's $10,000 in 3 months spend requirement, buy gift cards for places you already shop (grocery, Costco, Canadian Tire) at a grocery store — it counts as grocery spend and earns 2–3x points. You're pre-paying normal expenses while hitting the requirement naturally.

title: RBC Avion vs Amex Membership Rewards: Which Bank Currency Wins in 2026?
excerpt: Two of Canada's top flexible bank point currencies go head to head. We compare transfer partners, earn rates, redemption value, and who each program suits best.
heroImageAlt: Two premium credit cards side by side on a marble surface
metaDescription: RBC Avion vs Amex Membership Rewards — a full 2026 comparison of Canada's two best flexible bank currencies. Transfer partners, earn rates, and best use cases.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: Amex MR has more transfer partners and higher earn rates on everyday categories · RBC Avion is better for RBC banking clients and WestJet travellers · Amex MR wins for premium cabin travel flexibility · RBC Avion wins for simplicity and non-Amex card users

If you're choosing between the Amex ecosystem and RBC Avion Rewards, you're choosing between Canada's two most flexible bank point currencies. Both let you move points to airlines and hotels. But the partner lists, earn rates, and ideal use cases differ significantly. Here's the honest comparison.

content.heading: Transfer Partners: Amex MR Wins on Breadth

content.body: Amex MR's nine Canadian transfer partners versus Avion's five reflects a fundamental difference in strategy. Amex built a broad coalition; RBC focused on a tighter set with strong North American airline coverage. The absence of Aeroplan from RBC Avion is notable — if Aeroplan is your primary redemption target, Amex MR is the clear winner.

content.heading: Earn Rates: No Contest at the Top

content.body: The Amex Cobalt earns 5x MR on dining and groceries. The best RBC Avion card earns 1.25x Avion points on most purchases. This 4x difference in earn rate is not a rounding error — it's transformative. A household spending $2,000/month on food earns 10,000 Amex MR per month on the Cobalt (equivalent to 10,000 Aeroplan points) versus 2,500 Avion points on an equivalent RBC card. Over a year: 120,000 MR vs 30,000 Avion. The Cobalt's $155.88 annual fee is trivial relative to this difference.

content.heading: Where RBC Avion Has a Real Edge

items: WestJet redemptions: Avion transfers to WestJet Dollars at a reasonable rate — Amex MR cannot
American Airlines access: Avion → AAdvantage is unique to RBC; useful for US routing
Cathay Asia Miles: excellent for business class to Hong Kong and Asia — Amex MR lacks this partner
RBC banking integration: easier for existing RBC clients managing everything in one app
No Amex acceptance gaps: Avion is on Visa, accepted everywhere including Costco Canada

content.heading: Who Should Choose Amex MR

content.body: Choose Amex Membership Rewards if you fly Air Canada or Star Alliance primarily (transfer to Aeroplan), travel internationally to Europe (Avios, Flying Blue), want maximum flexibility across the most airline programs, or spend heavily on dining and groceries where the Cobalt's 5x earn rate is unmatched. Amex MR is also the better choice if you want to occasionally earn hotel points (Marriott Bonvoy, Hilton Honors) as a side benefit.

content.heading: Who Should Choose RBC Avion

content.body: Choose RBC Avion if you're a loyal WestJet flyer, want American Airlines access for US travel, prefer to keep your banking and credit card at one institution, or need a Visa card accepted at merchants that don't take Amex. Avion is also the right choice if your spending skews toward categories where Amex acceptance is occasionally spotty.

content.heading: Pro tip: Run both programs simultaneously
content.body: Nothing stops you from holding an Amex Cobalt (5x on food) and an RBC Avion Visa (1.25x everywhere else, plus WestJet/AAdvantage access). You get the highest possible earn on your biggest spend category, plus the unique redemption options only Avion offers. The two programs complement more than they compete.

title: Best Canadian Credit Cards for Groceries in 2026 — Ranked
excerpt: Groceries are most Canadians' largest monthly expense. Here are the credit cards that earn the most on every dollar spent at Sobeys, Loblaws, Metro, and Costco.
heroImageAlt: Person paying at a grocery store checkout with a credit card
metaDescription: Best Canadian credit cards for groceries in 2026. Compare earn rates at Loblaws, Sobeys, Metro, and Costco — from the Amex Cobalt to no-fee options.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: Amex Cobalt wins at 5x on all grocery stores · Scene+ Visa earns 3x at Sobeys-group stores specifically · Tangerine Money-Back is the best no-fee grocery card · Costco only accepts Mastercard — Rogers World Elite is best there

The average Canadian household spends $1,000–$1,400 per month on groceries. That's your largest discretionary expense — and a massive opportunity to earn points or cash back. But not all grocery credit cards are equal: some cards earn 5x at all grocers, others only at specific chains. Here's the definitive ranking for 2026.

content.heading: 1. Amex Cobalt — 5x on All Grocery Stores

content.body: The Amex Cobalt earns 5 Membership Rewards points per dollar at all Canadian grocery stores — Loblaws, Metro, Sobeys, Safeway, IGA, FreshCo, and more. At a household spending $1,200/month on groceries, that's 6,000 Amex MR per month or 72,000 per year. Those points transfer to Aeroplan at 1:1, making the Cobalt the single most powerful Aeroplan-earning card for non-travel spend in Canada. The $155.88 annual fee costs effectively nothing compared to the annual return — at 1.5¢ per Aeroplan point, that's $1,080 per year in Aeroplan value, or $720 used for a travel portal redemption at ~1¢.

content.heading: 2. Scotiabank Scene+ Visa — 3x at Sobeys Group

content.body: If you shop primarily at Sobeys, IGA, FreshCo, Foodland, or Safeway (all part of the Sobeys/Empire group), the Scotiabank Scene+ Visa earns 3x Scene+ points at these stores — and it has no annual fee. Scene+ points are worth 1¢ each redeemed for travel, so 3x = 3% return on Sobeys-group grocery spend. That rivals paid cards. The limit: it only earns 3x at Sobeys-banner stores. At Loblaws or Metro, it earns the base 1x.

content.heading: 3. Tangerine Money-Back — Best No-Fee Flexibility

content.body: The Tangerine Money-Back Mastercard lets you choose groceries as one of your 2% cash back categories, with no annual fee. You choose up to two (or three with a Tangerine savings account) categories for 2%, including groceries. On $1,200/month grocery spend, that's $24/month or $288/year in cash deposited to your account — completely automatically. Simple, transparent, no points to manage.

content.heading: 4. Costco: Rogers World Elite Mastercard

content.body: Costco Canada only accepts Mastercard. The Rogers World Elite Mastercard earns 1.5% cash back everywhere (including Costco), 3% on foreign currency purchases, and has no annual fee for qualifying customers ($80K+ personal income). For Costco regulars who spend $500+/month there, Rogers World Elite is the optimal choice — no other no-fee Mastercard comes close to 1.5% flat on all spend.

content.heading: 5. BMO eclipse Visa Infinite — 5x on Groceries and Dining

content.body: The BMO eclipse Visa Infinite also earns 5x BMO Rewards on groceries, dining, gas, and transit — at a $120 annual fee. BMO Rewards are worth approximately 0.67¢ per point redeemed for travel, giving you a ~3.35% return on grocery spend. The card also provides a $50 annual lifestyle credit that partially offsets the fee. For those who prefer Visa over Amex (for acceptance), the eclipse is the best Visa option for grocery earning.

content.heading: Pro tip: Use the Cobalt for all online grocery orders
content.body: The Amex Cobalt earns 5x on grocery orders placed through apps like Instacart, Walmart Grocery, PC Express, and Amazon Fresh — as long as the merchant category codes as 'grocery.' Test a small order first to confirm the coding, then redirect your entire grocery budget to the Cobalt for maximum earn.

title: ANA Business Class Using Aeroplan Points: The Complete Booking Guide
excerpt: ANA's 'The Room' is one of the world's best business class products. Here's exactly how Canadians can book it for 75,000 Aeroplan points one-way — and actually find award space.
heroImageAlt: Night view of Tokyo's illuminated skyline from above
metaDescription: How to book ANA business class 'The Room' using Aeroplan points. Step-by-step guide to finding availability and booking this world-class product for 75,000 points one-way.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: ANA The Room business class = 75,000 Aeroplan pts one-way from Canada · Widely ranked world's best business class · Award space is limited but does open 330 days out · Use the multi-city search on aeroplan.com to find the best routing

ANA 'The Room' business class has been voted the world's best business class product multiple times — fully-enclosed private suites, direct aisle access from every seat, and exceptional Japanese hospitality. In cash, a round trip in The Room from Toronto to Tokyo costs $12,000–$18,000. Using Aeroplan points, the same seat costs 75,000 points one-way. If you're going to redeem points for one aspirational trip in your life, this is one of the top candidates.

content.heading: What Makes ANA The Room Worth It

content.body: ANA introduced 'The Room' — their flagship business class suite — on the 777-9 and selected 777-300ER aircraft. Each seat is a fully-enclosed individual suite with a sliding door, lie-flat bed measuring 78 inches long, direct aisle access regardless of whether you're in a window or aisle seat (in a 1-2-1 configuration), a 24-inch 4K screen, and Wincs noise-cancelling headphones. The dining rivals business class at top hotels. Japanese seasonal menus are served on proper tableware. It is, without exaggeration, one of the finest travel experiences available on any airline at any price.

content.heading: The Aeroplan Award Chart for ANA Flights

content.body: Aeroplan uses a distance-based hybrid award chart. Flights from Canada (Zone 1) to Japan (Zone 11) price at 75,000 points one-way for a Saver business class award. The equivalent cash price on the same route regularly exceeds $6,000 one-way. At 75,000 points, you're extracting approximately 8 cents per point — the highest ratio of any commonly-available Aeroplan redemption. Economy class on the same route costs 40,000 Saver points.

content.heading: How to Find ANA Award Availability

content.body: ANA award space on Aeroplan is legitimately limited, but it exists and is findable. The best windows to search: 330 days before departure (when the booking window opens), and within 14 days of the flight (when ANA releases last-minute space to partners). Avoid peak Japanese travel dates: Golden Week (late April to early May), Obon (mid-August), and the cherry blossom season (late March to early April). Shoulder seasons — June, September, and November — tend to have the most award availability.

items: Search on aeroplan.com using the flexible dates calendar view
Try the multi-city tool to chain a short Air Canada segment with the ANA long-haul
Aim for direct routes: Toronto–Tokyo (YYZ–NRT) or Vancouver–Tokyo (YVR–NRT)
ANA also flies to Osaka (KIX) — sometimes better availability than NRT
If The Room aircraft aren't showing, check which aircraft type is scheduled using tools like SeatGuru or FlightAware

content.heading: How to Build 75,000 Aeroplan Points

content.body: The most efficient Canadian path: hold the Amex Cobalt and spend heavily on dining and groceries at 5x Amex MR (transfers 1:1 to Aeroplan). A household spending $1,200/month on food earns 72,000 MR in 12 months — nearly the full amount needed. Add a TD Aeroplan Visa Infinite welcome bonus of 20,000 points and you're over 90,000 before the year ends. Alternatively, the Amex Platinum's 70,000-point welcome bonus alone covers most of a round-trip redemption.

content.heading: Taxes and Fees on ANA Awards

content.body: ANA is one of the better partner airlines for fees and surcharges when booked through Aeroplan. Expect to pay approximately $80–$150 CAD in taxes and carrier fees on a one-way Canada–Japan award. By comparison, Lufthansa and British Airways awards can cost $400–$600 in surcharges for the same routes. The ANA surcharge is modest enough that it doesn't undermine the redemption value — it's one reason this award is so compelling.

content.heading: Pro tip: Book a stopover in Tokyo on a multi-destination award
content.body: Aeroplan allows one stopover of up to 24 hours on international one-way awards at no extra point cost. Route your Tokyo trip as Canada → Tokyo (stopover 20+ hours) → onward destination, and you essentially get Tokyo as a free bonus city on a one-way award. Common routing: YVR → NRT (stopover) → SIN (Singapore) for the same 75,000–85,000 points as a direct flight.

title: Scene+ Points in 2026: How to Earn and Redeem for Maximum Value
excerpt: Scene+ is Canada's most underrated coalition rewards program. Here's how to squeeze the most value from every point across Scotiabank, Sobeys, and Cineplex.
heroImageAlt: A neatly arranged workspace with notebook and financial planning materials
metaDescription: How to maximize Scene+ points in 2026. Earn at Scotiabank, Sobeys, and Cineplex — and redeem for travel, groceries, and movies at 1 cent per point.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: Scene+ points are worth exactly 1¢ each — simple and transparent · Best earning: 3x at Sobeys/IGA + 5x at Cineplex + up to 3x at Scotiabank · Redeem for travel through Scotiabank's travel portal (any booking, no blackout dates) · Stack with Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite for no foreign transaction fees

Scene+ doesn't get enough credit. While points enthusiasts obsess over Aeroplan and Amex MR, Scene+ has quietly become one of the most practical rewards programs in Canada — primarily because it's simple. Every point is worth exactly 1 cent. No award charts, no transfer partners, no blackout dates. Just accumulate and redeem at face value.

content.heading: The Scene+ Earning Ecosystem

content.body: Scene+ operates across three main partners: Scotiabank (banking and credit cards), the Sobeys grocery empire (Sobeys, IGA, Safeway, FreshCo, Foodland, Thrifty Foods), and Cineplex Entertainment. Beyond these anchors, Scene+ also earns at Home Hardware, Harvey's, Swiss Chalet, and a rotating list of retail partners. The program's grocery footprint is substantial — if you live in a Sobeys-banner market, almost every grocery dollar can earn Scene+ points.

content.heading: Best Ways to Earn Scene+ Points

items: Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite: 3x Scene+ on dining, 2x on groceries + transit + streaming — $150 annual fee
Scotiabank Scene+ Visa (no fee): 5x at Cineplex, 3x at Sobeys-group stores, 2x dining
Scotiabank Gold American Express: 6x at eligible grocery/dining/entertainment, 3x on gas and transit
Shopping directly at Sobeys-group stores with your Scene+ card: base 1x always stacks with credit card earn
Scene+ promotions: triple-point weekends at Sobeys, bonus points at Cineplex on new releases
Scotiabank banking: chequing and savings account bonus points for everyday banking activity

content.heading: Stacking Earn Rates: The Real Power Move

content.body: The real Scene+ magic happens through stacking. When you pay with a Scotiabank Scene+ Visa at Sobeys, you earn: 3x from the credit card, plus 1x from scanning your Scene+ app, plus any Sobeys-app bonus points running that week. During triple-point promotions, your credit card earn stays constant but your in-store earn triples from 1x to 3x. Regular Sobeys shoppers can routinely hit 6–9x total on grocery spend during promotional weekends.

content.heading: Best Redemption Options

content.body: Scene+ points are worth 1¢ each across all major redemption categories. For travel: redeem through Scotiabank's Expedia-powered travel portal — any flight, hotel, or car rental qualifies, with no blackout dates and no minimum redemption. 35,000 points = $350 toward any travel booking. You can also redeem against any travel purchase on your Scotiabank card within 12 months using the 'Pay with Points' feature. For everyday use: groceries at Sobeys, movies at Cineplex, and gift cards all redeem at 1¢/point.

content.heading: Scene+ vs Aeroplan: When to Choose Each

rows: For any destination via travel portal

content.heading: Pro tip: Load your Scene+ card before Sobeys triple-point events
content.body: Sobeys often announces triple-point weekends with only 3–5 days notice via the Scene+ app or Sobeys flyer. Enable app notifications so you're ready. Pre-plan a larger grocery run on these weekends — stocking up on shelf-stable items during a 9x total earn event is effectively getting a 9% discount on groceries.

title: Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite Review 2026: Still the Best Travel Card for No FX Fees?
excerpt: The Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite is one of Canada's best all-round travel cards. We break down whether the no-FX-fee benefit, lounge access, and earn rates justify the $150 annual fee in 2026.
heroImageAlt: Hand holding a credit card near a payment terminal at a cafe
metaDescription: Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite review for 2026. Is it worth the $150 annual fee? We evaluate the no-FX-fee benefit, 6 lounge visits, earn rates, and travel insurance.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: No foreign transaction fees save 2.5% on every overseas purchase — worth $250+ per $10,000 spent abroad · 6 free Visa Airport Companion lounge visits per year · 3x Scene+ on dining, 2x on groceries/transit/streaming · $150 annual fee breaks even if you travel internationally twice a year

Most Canadian credit cards charge a 2.5% foreign transaction fee on every purchase made in a foreign currency. If you spend $10,000 abroad per year — which any regular international traveller easily does — that's $250 quietly leaving your account. The Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite eliminates this fee entirely. For frequent international travellers, this single feature is enough to make the $150 annual fee worthwhile before counting any other benefits.

content.heading: The No-Foreign-Transaction-Fee Benefit: Real Dollar Value

content.body: The 2.5% FX fee on standard Canadian cards applies to everything charged in a non-Canadian currency: hotel bills in Europe, restaurant meals in the US, tour bookings in Southeast Asia, even online purchases from American retailers. At $10,000 in annual foreign spend, you're saving $250. At $20,000 — common for a couple on two international trips — you're saving $500. The $150 annual fee is fully offset by $6,000 in foreign currency spending per year.

content.heading: Earn Rates: Solid but Not Best-in-Class

items: 3x Scene+ points on dining and food delivery
2x on groceries, transit, streaming services, and eligible Scotiabank products
1x on all other purchases
Scene+ points worth 1¢ each = effectively 3% on dining, 2% on groceries/transit/streaming

content.body: These earn rates are respectable but not category-leading. The Amex Cobalt earns 5x on dining and groceries, which at 1.5¢/Aeroplan point is worth roughly 7.5% on food spend — more than double the Passport's 3%. However, the Passport is a Visa (accepted everywhere including Costco), whereas Amex still faces occasional acceptance gaps. If you want one card that works everywhere and earns well, the Passport is more convenient.

content.heading: 6 Annual Lounge Visits: What It's Worth

content.body: The Scotiabank Passport provides 6 complimentary visits per year to the Visa Airport Companion network — approximately 1,200 lounges at over 400 airports globally. Airport lounge access is typically $40–$60 per visit if purchased at the door. Six visits per year = $240–$360 in saved lounge fees. Combined with the FX fee savings, most international travellers recover the $150 annual fee many times over before even considering the earn rates.

content.heading: Travel Insurance Package

content.body: The Passport includes a competitive insurance package: 25 days of emergency travel medical coverage (up to $2 million), trip cancellation and interruption, flight delay, baggage delay and loss, and rental car collision/loss damage. The 25-day medical coverage is notably strong — many cards offer only 10–15 days. For longer trips, you'd need a top-up policy, but for most Canadian travel patterns this coverage is sufficient.

content.heading: Who Should Get the Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite

content.body: The Passport is the right card for Canadians who travel internationally at least twice per year, want a simple all-in-one card rather than a multi-card setup, need Visa acceptance everywhere (including Costco), and don't want to think about maximizing complex points programs. It's particularly strong for people who do significant online shopping from US retailers, where the FX savings apply on every purchase.

content.heading: Pro tip: Use the Passport for all US online shopping year-round
content.body: Amazon.com, American retailers, subscription services priced in USD — all these trigger the 2.5% FX fee on a standard Canadian card. Put them all on your Passport instead. A household spending $300/month on US-priced subscriptions and online shopping saves $90/year from FX fees alone, just on that category.

title: British Airways Avios for Canadians: The Complete Amex MR Transfer Guide
excerpt: Avios are one of the most underused tools in the Canadian points toolbox. Here's how to transfer Amex MR to BA Avios and find the best redemptions for Canadian-based travellers.
heroImageAlt: Airplane on a runway at dusk with lights illuminating the tarmac
metaDescription: How Canadians can use British Airways Avios from Amex MR transfers. Best Avios redemptions, the Iberia business class sweet spot, and how to find award space.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: Amex MR transfers to BA Avios at 1:1 in 3–5 days · Best Avios use for Canadians: Iberia business class to Madrid from 34,000 pts one-way · Short-haul flights under 500 miles = 4,500 Avios in economy · Avios pool across BA, Iberia, Aer Lingus, and Vueling

British Airways Avios doesn't get discussed enough in the Canadian points community, where Aeroplan dominates the conversation. But for specific redemptions — particularly short-haul flights, Iberia business class to Europe, and partner airline bookings — Avios offers value that Aeroplan simply cannot match. And since Amex MR transfers to Avios at 1:1, every Canadian holding an Amex Cobalt or Platinum has access.

content.heading: How Avios Work: The Distance Chart

content.body: Avios use a pure distance-based award chart — the further you fly, the more points you pay. This makes short flights extremely cheap (4,500 Avios for under 500 miles in economy) but long-haul flights expensive relative to other programs. The sweet spot is medium-haul redemptions in the 1,000–3,000 mile range, particularly within Europe, or on partner airlines where Avios offer lower cost than competing programs.

content.heading: The Iberia Sweet Spot: Best Avios Redemption for Canadians

content.body: The single best Avios redemption for Canadians: transfer Avios to Iberia Plus (Avios are shared between BA and Iberia, so they move instantly at 1:1), then book Iberia business class from New York or Boston to Madrid. The award costs 34,000 Avios one-way in Iberia business class — a seat that sells for $3,500–$6,000 in cash. Iberia's business class (flat-bed, direct aisle access on long-haul aircraft) is excellent, and the surcharges on Iberia awards are significantly lower than on BA-operated flights. Fly Air Canada or WestJet to JFK or BOS, then redeem Avios for the transatlantic leg.

content.heading: Short-Haul Redemptions: Cheapest in Points

content.body: Avios are uniquely efficient for short flights. The chart prices routes under 500 miles at 4,500 Avios in economy — routes like Toronto to New York, Toronto to Montreal, or Vancouver to Seattle. At a cash value of $200–$400 per ticket, you're getting excellent value per Avios. This makes Avios perfect for topping off with a quick domestic segment, or for families booking multiple short-haul seats where the Avios cost stays low.

content.heading: Partner Airlines: Booking Non-BA Flights with Avios

content.body: Avios can be used to book flights on all oneworld alliance carriers — American Airlines, Cathay Pacific, Finnair, Japan Airlines, Malaysia Airlines, and more. Japan Airlines business class to Tokyo can be booked for around 55,000 Avios one-way — competitive with Aeroplan's 75,000 points for ANA. Cathay Pacific business class to Hong Kong runs approximately 75,000 Avios, also strong value. The availability and pricing vary by carrier.

content.heading: Fuel Surcharges: The Big Caveat on BA Flights

content.body: British Airways charges significant fuel surcharges on award flights operated by BA itself — commonly $400–$600 CAD per round trip in business class. This dramatically reduces the value of BA-operated awards. The workaround: book partner airline flights using Avios instead. Japan Airlines, Iberia, Finnair, and American Airlines awards booked through Avios carry much lower or no surcharges. Always check the total cost before booking — an Avios award on BA itself may be worse value than booking direct or using Aeroplan.

content.heading: Pro tip: Pool Avios across household members for a big redemption
content.body: BA and Iberia allow Avios pooling within a household account. If you and your partner both hold Amex cards and have accumulated 40,000 Avios each, pool them to 80,000 and book two seats to Madrid in Iberia business class for 68,000 Avios total — flights that would cost $12,000+ in cash. Avios pooling is one of the most powerful underused features in Canadian travel rewards.

title: TD vs CIBC Aeroplan Visa Infinite: Which Card Should Canadians Choose?
excerpt: Both cards earn Aeroplan directly and carry similar fees. Here's what actually differs — and which one makes more sense depending on your bank, travel style, and goals.
heroImageAlt: Two open notebooks side by side on a clean white desk
metaDescription: TD Aeroplan Visa Infinite vs CIBC Aeroplan Visa Infinite — a complete 2026 comparison. Earn rates, insurance, perks, and which card wins for Canadian Aeroplan collectors.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: Both earn identical Aeroplan rates on most categories · TD has stronger travel insurance (emergency medical up to 21 days) · CIBC edges ahead for Air Canada perks (preferred pricing, priority boarding) · Get both if you want to earn Aeroplan faster across two households

The TD Aeroplan Visa Infinite and CIBC Aeroplan Visa Infinite are remarkably similar cards — both earn Aeroplan miles at the same rate, carry the same annual fee, and are issued by two of Canada's Big Five banks. But the differences matter, and choosing the right one depends on where you bank, how often you fly Air Canada, and which insurance coverage matters most to you.

content.heading: Earn Rates: Essentially Identical

content.body: The earn rates are a tie — both cards offer 1.5x Aeroplan on groceries, gas, and Air Canada purchases. This is the core Aeroplan earning proposition. Both cards also give you 500 bonus Aeroplan points the first time you make a purchase each month, an incentive to use the card regularly. On this dimension alone, there's no meaningful difference.

content.heading: Travel Insurance: TD Has the Edge

content.body: TD's travel insurance package is marginally stronger, particularly for emergency medical coverage. TD provides up to $2 million in emergency travel medical insurance for up to 21 days per trip. CIBC provides similar coverage but with a 15-day limit per trip for cardholders under 65. If you frequently take trips longer than 15 days — extended holidays, long-haul Asia or South America trips — TD's coverage is meaningfully better without requiring a top-up policy.

content.heading: Air Canada Perks: CIBC Has the Edge

content.body: CIBC edges ahead for loyalty to Air Canada specifically. CIBC Aeroplan cardholders get preferred pricing on select Air Canada vacation packages and, at higher card tiers (CIBC Aeroplan Visa Infinite Privilege), priority check-in, preferred boarding, and a free checked bag. For frequent Air Canada flyers who don't hold elite Aeroplan status, these perks have real value — particularly the first free checked bag at $35 per segment.

content.heading: Welcome Bonus: Watch for Promotions

content.body: Both TD and CIBC run competing welcome bonus offers throughout the year, often with annual fee rebates for the first year. The bonus structures change frequently — sometimes TD leads, sometimes CIBC. When comparing, always factor in the net first-year value (welcome bonus minus annual fee if not waived). Both have offered 20,000–40,000 Aeroplan point bonuses in strong promotional periods.

content.heading: The Practical Choice: Which Bank Do You Use?

content.body: For most Canadians, the deciding factor is simpler than the feature breakdown: which bank do you already use for chequing and savings? Both TD and CIBC offer relationship benefits — slightly better welcome offer access, combined statement viewing, and occasionally waived fees — to existing banking clients. If you bank at TD, go with TD Aeroplan Visa Infinite. If you bank at CIBC, get CIBC Aeroplan Visa Infinite. The earn rates won't differ enough to justify switching banks.

content.heading: Pro tip: Household strategy — get both cards
content.body: Since both cards are from different banks, a couple can hold one of each. Partner 1 holds the TD Aeroplan Visa Infinite; Partner 2 holds the CIBC Aeroplan Visa Infinite. Both earn Aeroplan, and since Aeroplan allows free account sharing between household members, you pool into one bucket. Your household effectively earns Aeroplan on two sets of welcome bonuses and two streams of ongoing spend — doubling your accumulation rate.

title: Best Canadian Credit Cards for Dining and Restaurants in 2026
excerpt: Whether you're eating out twice a week or ordering delivery every night, these are the Canadian cards that earn the most on restaurant spend — from premium to no-fee options.
heroImageAlt: Elegant restaurant interior with warm lighting and set tables
metaDescription: Best Canadian credit cards for dining and restaurants in 2026. From the Amex Cobalt's 5x to no-fee options — ranked by real annual value for Canadians.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: Amex Cobalt leads with 5x MR on dining — unmatched in Canada · Scotiabank Passport earns 3x Scene+ on dining with no FX fees · Rogers World Elite earns 1.5% flat cash back everywhere including restaurants · Tangerine 2% is best no-fee dining card with category choice flexibility

The average Canadian household spends $400–$700 per month on restaurants, takeout, and food delivery. That's a significant spend category, and the credit card you use for it makes a real difference. Here are the cards that earn the most on every meal out — ranked honestly.

content.heading: 1. Amex Cobalt — 5x on All Dining and Delivery

content.body: No Canadian card comes close to the Amex Cobalt's 5x Membership Rewards on dining. This includes sit-down restaurants, fast food, food delivery apps (Uber Eats, DoorDash, Skip), coffee shops, and food trucks — anything coded as 'food and beverage' or 'restaurant.' At a household spending $500/month on dining and delivery, that's 2,500 MR points per month or 30,000 per year from dining alone. At 1.5¢/Aeroplan point (after transferring), that's $450 in annual travel value from restaurant spend. The $155.88 annual fee is easily recovered.

content.heading: 2. Scotiabank Gold American Express — 6x at Eligible Dining

content.body: The Scotiabank Gold Amex technically leads the earn rate race at 6x Scene+ on eligible grocery, dining, and entertainment purchases combined, with a $120 annual fee. However, the 6x is a combined category — dining, grocery, and eligible entertainment all share the same earn tier. Standalone dining earn is listed at 5x on restaurant and food delivery, making it comparable to the Cobalt in practice. Scene+ points at 1¢ each = 5% return on dining. The advantage over Cobalt: no FX fees on foreign purchases (including overseas dining).

content.heading: 3. Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite — 3x on Dining, No FX Fees

content.body: For travellers who use their card heavily while overseas, the Scotiabank Passport Visa Infinite earns 3x Scene+ on dining — a 3% return — and charges no foreign transaction fees. If you dine out abroad frequently, the FX fee savings (2.5% on every foreign transaction) effectively add another 2.5% on top of your 3% dining earn. Combined, it's 5.5% effective return on overseas restaurant spend — better than any card for international dining.

content.heading: 4. Tangerine Money-Back — Best No-Fee Dining Card

content.body: The Tangerine Money-Back Mastercard allows you to choose restaurants as one of your 2% cash back categories, at zero annual fee. On $500/month dining spend, that's $10/month or $120/year in cash deposited automatically. It's not the highest earn rate, but for cardholders who want simplicity and no annual fee, Tangerine's dining category is the best no-fee option in Canada. The cash back deposits to your account monthly — no redemption required.

content.heading: 5. Rogers World Elite Mastercard — Best for Consistent Cash Back

content.body: The Rogers World Elite Mastercard earns 1.5% cash back on all purchases — including dining — with no annual fee (for qualifying income). It's not the top earn rate for restaurants specifically, but it's a Mastercard accepted everywhere (including Tim Hortons and local restaurants that don't take Amex), and the 1.5% flat rate applies whether you're dining in Canada or abroad. The 3% rate on foreign currency purchases is also the highest available on any no-fee Canadian card for overseas dining.

content.heading: Pro tip: Register your Cobalt for Amex Offers on food delivery
content.body: Amex frequently runs targeted Offers for Cobalt cardholders on Uber Eats, DoorDash, and Skip — often 10–15% statement credits or bonus points on food delivery orders. Combined with the 5x base earn, these promotions can push your effective return on food delivery to 15–20%. Check your Amex app under 'Offers' regularly and activate any food-related offers before ordering.

title: Flying Blue Promo Rewards: How Canadians Book Air France Business Class for 50,000 Points
excerpt: Air France's monthly Promo Rewards can slash business class award prices by 25–50%. Here's how Canadians can use Amex MR transfers to take advantage of these deals.
heroImageAlt: Aerial view of Paris with the Eiffel Tower and the Seine River at sunset
metaDescription: How Canadians use Air France Flying Blue Promo Rewards to book business class to Europe for 50,000 points. Using Amex MR transfers to access monthly promotional awards.

content.heading: TL;DR
content.body: Flying Blue Promo Rewards drop 25–50% on select routes every month on the 15th · Amex MR transfers to Flying Blue at 1:1 in 3–5 days · Air France business class to Paris has hit 45,000–55,000 miles one-way during promos · KLM routes to Amsterdam often have strong promo availability

Air France-KLM Flying Blue runs one of the most interesting promotions in travel rewards: every month, they publish a list of routes with award prices reduced by 25–50%. When one of those routes aligns with where you want to go, you can book business class to Europe for significantly fewer miles than the standard rate. For Canadians with Amex MR points, the 1:1 transfer makes this accessible within days.

content.heading: What Are Flying Blue Promo Rewards?

content.body: Flying Blue Promo Rewards are monthly discounts applied to specific routes and fare classes. The promotion releases on the 15th of each month and typically runs for 30 days. Routes can include Air France, KLM, and their Skyteam partners (Delta, Virgin Atlantic, Air Europa, and others). The discounts range from 25% to 50% — meaning a business class route that normally costs 80,000 miles might drop to 40,000–60,000 during a promo. The catch: these are fixed routes released each month, and you can't predict which ones will appear.

content.heading: How to Access the Promos

items: Create a free Flying Blue account at flyingblue.com
Subscribe to the Flying Blue newsletter — promos are announced on the 15th
Visit flyingblue.com/en/promo-rewards on the 15th to see the new list
Check for Canada-origin routes (Montreal, Toronto, Vancouver) to Paris CDG or Amsterdam AMS
Once you see a useful route, transfer Amex MR to Flying Blue immediately (transfer takes 3–5 days)
Book the promo award before seats sell out — popular routes go fast

content.heading: Air France Business Class: What You're Actually Getting

content.body: Air France's long-haul business class product is competitive — flat-bed seats in a 2-2-2 herringbone configuration on the 777 (direct aisle access from all window seats), and their newer 'New Business' product in a 1-2-1 direct-aisle-access layout on select aircraft. The cabin service is excellent, with proper glassware, multi-course meals, and attentive service. For a route like Montreal–Paris (a common promo target), you're booking a 7-8 hour overnight flight in a flat bed for 45,000–55,000 miles during strong promo periods.

content.heading: How to Transfer Amex MR to Flying Blue

content.body: Transfer your Amex MR points to Flying Blue via the American Express Canada website. Navigate to 'Transfer Points,' select Flying Blue as the destination, enter your Flying Blue account number, and specify the amount. The transfer processes in 3–5 business days. The ratio is 1:1 — 50,000 Amex MR becomes 50,000 Flying Blue miles. This is why acting fast on Promo Rewards is critical: you need to see the promo, initiate the transfer, wait for it to land, then book — all within the promotional window.

content.heading: Fuel Surcharges on Flying Blue Awards

content.body: Air France and KLM do charge fuel surcharges on award tickets, but they're moderate compared to British Airways — typically $150–$300 CAD one-way in business class from North America. This is well within acceptable range for a business class award, especially since you're redeeming promo-priced miles. KLM routes sometimes carry lower surcharges than Air France, so check both when available.

content.heading: Planning Your Flying Blue Strategy

content.body: The unpredictable nature of Promo Rewards means you can't plan precisely months in advance. The best approach: accumulate a buffer of Flying Blue miles (or Amex MR that can be quickly transferred), monitor the 15th of each month, and have flexible travel dates. Promo awards are particularly common for shoulder-season travel — September to November and February to March — when airlines have more unsold business class inventory to offer.

content.heading: Pro tip: Keep 60,000 Amex MR in reserve specifically for Flying Blue promos
content.body: Designate a portion of your Amex MR balance as your 'Flying Blue reserve.' Don't transfer it out, don't redeem it elsewhere — just hold it. When an excellent promo drops on the 15th, you can transfer immediately and book within the week. You're not locked in to Aeroplan — the flexibility to pivot to Flying Blue on short notice is exactly why Amex MR is the most powerful currency in Canada.
